stories.headline: Trump claims 'productive' Iran talks and postpones power plant strikes, but Tehran denies any contact
stories.body: President Trump announced he had ordered the Pentagon to postpone military strikes against Iran's power plants and energy infrastructure for five days, citing 'very good and productive conversations' with a 'top person' in Iran. However, Tehran flatly denied any talks had taken place. Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf called Trump's claims 'fake news' used to 'manipulate the financial and oil markets and escape the quagmire in which the US and Israel are trapped.' Iran's foreign ministry said only that messages had been received from friendly countries regarding the US's request for negotiations. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu said Trump believes there is an opportunity to leverage the war to reach an agreement, while adding that Israel continues to attack both in Iran and Lebanon. The postponement came just hours before Trump's 48-hour ultimatum to Iran was set to expire, providing what analysts described as an offramp from an escalation that had sent global markets into turmoil.

stories.headline: Colombian military transport plane crashes after takeoff, killing at least 66
stories.body: A Colombian Air Force C-130 Hercules carrying 121 soldiers and 11 crew crashed shortly after takeoff from Puerto Leguizamo in the country's southern Amazon region, killing at least 66 people and injuring dozens more. Ammunition on board detonated in the resulting fire, complicating rescue operations. Locals transported injured soldiers to hospitals on motorbikes as emergency services struggled to reach the remote area near the Peruvian border. President Gustavo Petro described it as a 'horrendous accident that should not have happened' and blamed bureaucratic delays for holding up his plans to modernise the military fleet. Defence Minister Pedro Sanchez said the aircraft was in airworthy condition and ruled out an attack by armed groups. The incident was one of the deadliest in the history of Colombia's air force.

stories.headline: Meloni concedes defeat as Italians reject judicial reform in referendum
stories.body: Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni conceded defeat after voters rejected her signature judicial reform package in a constitutional referendum, with the 'No' camp winning roughly 53.5 percent to 46.5 percent on turnout exceeding 58 percent. The reform would have separated the roles of judges and prosecutors, restructured the Superior Council of the Judiciary by replacing peer elections with a lottery system, and created a new disciplinary court. Critics, including more than 80 percent of Italy's magistrates who went on strike over the proposals, called it a political power grab that would weaken judicial independence. Meloni called the result 'a lost opportunity to modernise Italy' but vowed to continue governing, saying the defeat does not change her commitment to the nation. Opposition leader Elly Schlein of the Democratic Party hailed the result as a victory for constitutional safeguards.

stories.headline: Japan begins biggest-ever release from strategic oil reserves as energy crisis deepens
stories.body: Japan will begin releasing about 80 million barrels of oil from its strategic reserves this week, the largest such drawdown in the country's history and 1.8 times the quantity released after the 2011 Fukushima disaster. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said the release, equivalent to 45 days of domestic demand, will begin on Thursday and supplement 15 days of private-sector reserves already approved. Japan imports more than 90 percent of its crude from the Middle East, making it acutely vulnerable to disruption of the Strait of Hormuz. The government has introduced subsidies to cap gasoline at about 170 yen per litre after retail prices hit a record 190.8 yen. Consumer anxiety has triggered hoarding of toilet paper despite the trade ministry pointing out that 97 percent of it is made domestically from recycled materials.

stories.headline: Hungarian foreign minister allegedly fed EU Council secrets to Russia for years
stories.body: The European Union faces a serious internal security crisis after reports by The Washington Post revealed that Hungary's Foreign Minister Peter Szijjarto allegedly spent years leaking confidential European Council discussions to Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov during breaks in Brussels meetings. The practice reportedly gave Moscow advance knowledge of EU sanctions, individual member states' positions, and internal divisions. The European Commission called the reports 'extremely worrying' and demanded urgent clarification from Budapest. EU diplomats confirmed that Hungary has already been excluded from some high-level working groups on military intelligence, cybersecurity, and Ukraine logistics. Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk said Poland had long suspected the activity, noting that Hungarian positions 'suspiciously aligned' with Kremlin needs. The investigation also uncovered alleged Russian proposals to stage fake assassination attempts against Hungarian politicians to mobilise votes for Orban's party.

stories.headline: Two pilots killed as Air Canada jet collides with fire truck at LaGuardia Airport
stories.body: An Air Canada regional jet carrying more than 70 passengers from Montreal collided with a Port Authority fire truck on a runway at New York's LaGuardia Airport late Sunday night, killing both pilots and injuring 41 people. Air traffic control audio captured a voice saying 'Truck One, stop, stop, stop!' seconds before the crash, which crushed the nose of the aircraft. The fire truck had been dispatched minutes earlier to another plane that reported an odour issue. Passengers described a rough landing followed by a loud boom, with some escaping by sliding down the plane's wing. One of the pilots was identified as Antoine Forest, a 30-year-old from Quebec. The NTSB has opened an investigation, and Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy visited the site, emphasising that seatbelts saved lives. The runway is expected to remain closed for days.

stories.headline: Senate nears deal to end DHS shutdown as Mullin confirmed as new secretary
stories.body: Senators from both parties worked through the night on a potential breakthrough to end the monthlong Department of Homeland Security shutdown, with a proposal to fund most of the department including TSA airport workers but exclude ICE enforcement operations at the centre of the dispute. Republican senators met with Trump at the White House, and Senate leaders from both parties described the discussions as 'very positive and productive.' Separately, the Senate confirmed former Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin as the new DHS secretary in a 54-45 vote, replacing Kristi Noem after months of bipartisan criticism. Mullin, a former mixed martial arts fighter, promised a 'better approach' on immigration enforcement. The developments came as TSA agents continued to work without pay and ICE officers were deployed to major airports, where security lines stretched for hours during peak spring travel season.

stories.headline: Record-shattering March heat wave spreads eastward across the US
stories.body: A massive heat dome that has already broken March temperature records in 14 states is expanding eastward and may become one of the most expansive heat waves in American history. On Friday, four locations in Arizona and California hit 112 degrees Fahrenheit (44.4 Celsius), smashing the previous March record for the continental US by 4 degrees and coming within 1 degree of the all-time April record. Flagstaff, Arizona has seen 11 or 12 straight days above its previous March record. The National Weather Service said temperatures in the 90s Fahrenheit would spread across the southern and central Plains by Wednesday, with up to a third of the continental states flirting with March records. Weather historians said the physical area of the heat wave likely dwarfs the major events of 2012 and 2021, though its March timing and lower humidity make it less dangerous to human health than summer heat.

stories.headline: Hong Kong police can now demand phone passwords under amended national security law
stories.body: Hong Kong police can now demand that anyone suspected of breaching the city's national security law provide mobile phone or computer passwords, under amendments published on Monday that bypassed the legislature entirely. Refusing to comply carries up to one year in jail and a fine of up to HK$100,000, while providing false information could bring three years' imprisonment. The amendments also empower customs officers to seize items deemed to have 'seditious intention' without any arrest having been made. A UK-based law lecturer called the powers 'grossly disproportionate,' noting they require no judicial authorisation. To date, 386 people have been arrested under Hong Kong's national security law, with 176 individuals and four companies convicted, including media tycoon Jimmy Lai, who was sentenced to 20 years in February.

stories.headline: Moreno calls Andalusian elections for 17 May as campaign cycle continues
stories.body: Andalusian President Juanma Moreno announced the dissolution of the regional parliament and called elections for 17 May, continuing a cycle of early regional votes that has already seen PP victories in Extremadura, Aragon, and Castilla y Leon. Moreno said the date would maximise participation and allow Andalusia to reach summer with 'a clear political horizon.' The PP hopes to retain its absolute majority, though the open question is whether Moreno can avoid dependence on Vox, as has occurred in the other three regions where deals have yet to be struck. The PSOE will field Finance Minister Maria Jesus Montero as its candidate, with her resignation from the national government expected imminently. To the left of the Socialists, Podemos, Sumar, and other parties have yet to resolve their formula for running.

stories.headline: PP threatens to vote down government's crisis decree unless income tax is adjusted for inflation
stories.body: The PP is considering voting against the government's emergency economic decree addressing the Iran war's impact, just two days before the congressional vote, unless Pedro Sanchez commits to deflating personal income tax brackets to account for inflation. Sources close to PP leader Alberto Nunez Feijoo told El Mundo that voting no is now a real option, accusing the government of inserting unrelated budgetary provisions and a clause they interpret as hastening nuclear plant closures. The party's parliamentary spokesperson sent a letter to the presidency minister laying out conditions, while Podemos has announced it will abstain and Junts has not revealed its position. Without support from either the PP or Junts, the decree containing fuel and energy tax cuts could fail, leaving current relief measures at risk of expiring.

stories.headline: Strasbourg court rejects final appeal by Catalan independence leaders over pre-trial detention
stories.body: The European Court of Human Rights has declined to send the case of Oriol Junqueras, Jordi Turull, and Jordi Sanchez to its Grand Chamber for review, making final its November ruling that their pre-trial imprisonment during the independence crisis was not politically motivated. The decision by the Strasbourg court validates the actions of Supreme Court magistrate Pablo Llarena and the trial chamber, concluding that the three had legal and constitutional means to express their political views and that their detention was proportionate given the gravity of the offences. The ruling effectively closes the last international legal avenue for the three convicted pro-independence leaders to challenge their imprisonment.

stories.headline: PP files criminal complaint against state pollster chief Tezanos for alleged electoral manipulation
stories.body: The PP announced it will file a criminal complaint against Jose Felix Tezanos, president of Spain's Centre for Sociological Research (CIS), for an alleged electoral offence. PP institutional affairs secretary Cuca Gamarra accused Tezanos of a 'deliberate and partisan use' of the public polling body, noting that CIS surveys consistently place the PSOE ahead of the PP by wide margins that contradict every private polling firm. The latest CIS barometer estimated Sanchez would win a general election with 8.5 points more than Feijoo. Gamarra alleged the CIS employs an unvalidated methodology whose sole purpose is to 'systematically inflate the governing party,' calling it 'corruption' funded with public money. The complaint, based partly on a Senate investigation the PP led into the CIS, will be filed in Madrid courts in the coming days.

stories.headline: Puig and Estee Lauder in merger talks to create cosmetics giant
stories.body: Spanish beauty conglomerate Puig, owner of Jean Paul Gaultier and Rabanne, is in negotiations for a potential merger with American cosmetics giant Estee Lauder, owner of MAC, Clinique, and Jo Malone. The deal, first reported by the Financial Times and confirmed by multiple Spanish outlets, would create one of the world's largest luxury beauty groups. Estee Lauder's owner Leonid Radvinsky died last week at age 43, and the company has been exploring strategic options. Puig, based in Barcelona and listed on the Spanish stock exchange since 2024, reported revenues of over 4.3 billion euros last year. The talks are described as preliminary and no agreement is guaranteed.

stories.headline: International drug trafficking ring dismantled with 19 arrests across Catalunya, Malaga, and Bulgaria
stories.body: Mossos d'Esquadra and the National Police arrested 19 people in a major international operation coordinated by Europol against a criminal organisation involved in arms and drug trafficking, primarily marijuana. Fourteen of the arrests took place in Catalunya, in locations including Teia, Barcelona, Matarot, Sabadell, Tordera, and Lloret de Mar, with others in Malaga and Bulgaria, plus six people under investigation in Greece. Over 200 police officers were deployed in the operation, which included raids on properties in Barcelona's Poble-sec neighbourhood and a restaurant in the Barceloneta. Weapons, cash, and drugs were seized. The investigation, overseen by a Tortosa court since July 2025, remains open and further arrests are not ruled out.

stories.headline: Barcelona to start fining unauthorised vehicles on Via Laietana from 27 April
stories.body: Barcelona will begin automatically fining vehicles that drive up the Via Laietana without authorisation from 27 April, when camera-based enforcement goes live on the renovated artery. Residents can register their vehicles for access permits starting this week through an online portal or at citizen service offices. The renovation reduced the uphill direction to a single lane restricted to authorised vehicles including residents, emergency services, public transport, and delivery vehicles, creating more space for pedestrians, cyclists, and buses. Unauthorised vehicles caught using the ascending lane will face fines of 90 euros. The Via Laietana project has been one of the most significant changes to traffic flow in the Ciutat Vella district.

stories.headline: 19-year-old woman dies of suspected drug overdose in Sant Andreu encampment
stories.body: A 19-year-old woman was found dead on Sunday afternoon in an informal encampment of about 15 shacks on Carrer Gran de Sant Andreu in Barcelona. Mossos d'Esquadra said the death is linked to drug use combined with medication and have ruled out foul play. The Guardia Urbana was first to respond after receiving a call around 3:50 PM. Neighbours told local media the encampment was established just over a year ago and has been the scene of frequent disputes, with police called twice in the past week alone. Barcelona currently has 536 people living in encampments and squatted buildings across the city, of whom 18 percent are minors.

stories.headline: Palamos inaugurates newly renovated seafront promenade
stories.body: The Costa Brava town of Palamos will officially open its completely renovated Passeig del Mar on Friday evening with a ceremony featuring the town's giants and traditional gralla musicians, followed by a performance by the Cobla Badia de Palamos. The inauguration marks the completion of a comprehensive overhaul of the seafront walkway, one of the most significant urban improvement projects in the Baix Emporda town in recent years.

stories.headline: Catalan government approves 400 million euro package to cushion Iran war's economic impact
stories.body: President Salvador Illa announced that the Catalan government will approve a package of 400 million euros in measures to mitigate the economic effects of the war in the Middle East, including interest rate subsidies of up to 1.5 percent for self-employed workers and businesses affected by rising energy costs. The measures come as the regional government also prepares to expand its automatic licence plate reading camera network to more roads and municipalities across Catalunya, part of broader security and infrastructure investments.
